title: Tense Mistakes - 50 Mistakes
definition: Tense mistakes happen when the verb form doesn't match the time being described, or when a sentence mixes two different tenses that don't belong together. Here are 50 of the most common tense mistakes, explained simply, with a clear way to fix each one and avoid it in the future.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am knowing the answer.
commonMistakes.right: I know the answer.
commonMistakes.why: 'Know' is a state verb - it describes a mental state, not an action - so it's not normally used with '-ing'. Other state verbs to remember are: like, want, need, believe, and understand. As a rule, ask yourself: is this something you're doing, or something you simply are/feel? If it's a feeling or state, avoid '-ing'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I have seen him yesterday.
commonMistakes.right: I saw him yesterday.
commonMistakes.why: The present perfect tense ('have seen') is for actions with no specific time mentioned, but 'yesterday' is a specific point in the past, so you need the simple past ('saw') instead. Whenever a sentence includes a specific past time word (yesterday, last week, in 2020), always use the simple past tense.

commonMistakes.wrong: She is living here since 2015.
commonMistakes.right: She has been living here since 2015.
commonMistakes.why: The word 'since' signals an action that started in the past and is still continuing now, which requires the present perfect continuous tense ('has been living'), not the present continuous. Whenever you see 'since' or 'for' describing duration up to now, reach for 'have/has been + verb-ing'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am living in this city for ten years.
commonMistakes.right: I have been living in this city for ten years.
commonMistakes.why: Just like with 'since', the word 'for' describing a duration up to the present moment needs the present perfect continuous tense. Present continuous ('am living') only describes what's happening right now, not a duration connecting past to present.

commonMistakes.wrong: He goed to school yesterday.
commonMistakes.right: He went to school yesterday.
commonMistakes.why: 'Go' is an irregular verb, meaning its past tense doesn't simply add '-ed' - you must memorize the special form 'went'. Whenever a verb feels irregular, it's worth keeping a small personal list of these forms rather than guessing.

commonMistakes.wrong: Did you went there?
commonMistakes.right: Did you go there?
commonMistakes.why: After the helping verb 'did', the main verb must return to its base form, not stay in the past tense - 'did' already shows the sentence is in the past. A simple rule: 'did' + base verb, never 'did' + past-tense verb.

commonMistakes.wrong: She didn't went to work.
commonMistakes.right: She didn't go to work.
commonMistakes.why: Just like with 'did', the negative form 'didn't' already carries the past meaning, so the main verb after it must stay in its base form. Never put two past-tense markers in one verb phrase - choose either 'didn't' or the past form, not both.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am agree with you.
commonMistakes.right: I agree with you.
commonMistakes.why: 'Agree' is a regular action verb, not something that needs 'am/is/are' in front of it - many learners mistakenly add 'am' out of habit from other structures. Simply use 'agree' directly after the subject, with no extra helping verb.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am understanding your point.
commonMistakes.right: I understand your point.
commonMistakes.why: Like 'know', 'understand' is a state verb describing a mental condition, not an ongoing action, so it doesn't take '-ing' in standard English. Whenever you notice a verb describing thinking, feeling, or possessing (understand, believe, own, belong), avoid the continuous form.

commonMistakes.wrong: By the time I arrived, she left.
commonMistakes.right: By the time I arrived, she had left.
commonMistakes.why: When two things happened in the past, and one happened before the other, the earlier action needs the past perfect tense ('had left') to show it came first. Whenever you're describing which of two past events happened first, put 'had' before the earlier one.

commonMistakes.wrong: I will call you when I will arrive.
commonMistakes.right: I will call you when I arrive.
commonMistakes.why: In time clauses starting with 'when', 'after', 'before', or 'as soon as', English uses the present tense to describe a future event, not 'will'. Remember: only one 'will' is needed per sentence, in the main clause - the time clause stays in present tense even though it refers to the future.

commonMistakes.wrong: If it will rain, I will stay home.
commonMistakes.right: If it rains, I will stay home.
commonMistakes.why: Just like time clauses, 'if' clauses describing a real future possibility use the present tense, not 'will' - this is called the First Conditional. Keep the 'will' only in the result part of the sentence, never in the 'if' part.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am used to wake up early.
commonMistakes.right: I am used to waking up early.
commonMistakes.why: The phrase 'used to' has two different meanings and forms: 'used to + base verb' describes a past habit ('I used to wake up early'), while 'be used to + -ing' describes something familiar or normal now. Since this sentence uses 'am' (present), it needs the -ing form: 'used to waking up'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am having a car.
commonMistakes.right: I have a car.
commonMistakes.why: When 'have' means possession (owning something), it's a state verb and doesn't take the continuous form. 'Having' is only correct when it means 'experiencing' something, like 'I am having a great time' - possession itself should stay simple.

commonMistakes.wrong: She has gone to market yesterday.
commonMistakes.right: She went to the market yesterday.
commonMistakes.why: Just like the earlier mistake with 'seen', specific past time words like 'yesterday' require the simple past tense, not the present perfect. If you can name exactly when something happened, use simple past, not 'has/have + past participle'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am not knowing what to do.
commonMistakes.right: I don't know what to do.
commonMistakes.why: Since 'know' is a state verb, it should never appear in a continuous ('-ing') form, even in the negative. The correct negative pattern for state verbs is simply 'don't/doesn't + base verb'.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is having two brothers.
commonMistakes.right: He has two brothers.
commonMistakes.why: Describing family relationships or possessions with 'have' is a state, not an action, so no '-ing' form is used. This exact mistake is extremely common, so it's worth practicing 'has two brothers/sisters' as a fixed, memorized phrase.

commonMistakes.wrong: I will have finished by tomorrow, I think.
commonMistakes.right: I will have finished by tomorrow.
commonMistakes.why: This sentence is grammatically fine, but a very common related error is dropping 'have' entirely, saying 'I will finished' - always keep the full structure 'will have + past participle' together as one unit for actions completed before a future point.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am liking this song.
commonMistakes.right: I like this song.
commonMistakes.why: 'Like' is a state verb describing a feeling, not an ongoing action, so standard English avoids the continuous form (some informal marketing slogans use it for effect, but it's not grammatically standard). Stick with simple present for feelings: like, love, hate, prefer.

commonMistakes.wrong: When I was young, I use to play outside.
commonMistakes.right: When I was young, I used to play outside.
commonMistakes.why: 'Used to' (with a 'd') describes a past habit that no longer happens - many learners drop the 'd' when writing because it sounds the same when spoken. Always remember the 'd' in writing: u-s-e-d to.

commonMistakes.wrong: I have finish my homework.
commonMistakes.right: I have finished my homework.
commonMistakes.why: The present perfect tense needs the past participle form of the verb ('finished'), not the base form ('finish'). Always double-check that the verb after 'have/has' is in its correct past participle form.

commonMistakes.wrong: She has wrote three books.
commonMistakes.right: She has written three books.
commonMistakes.why: 'Write' is irregular, and its past participle is 'written', not 'wrote' (which is the simple past form). It helps to memorize irregular verbs in three forms together: write, wrote, written.

commonMistakes.wrong: I seen that movie already.
commonMistakes.right: I have seen that movie already.
commonMistakes.why: 'Seen' is the past participle of 'see' and can never be used alone as a main verb - it always needs a helping verb like 'have' or 'has' before it. Whenever you use a past participle (seen, done, gone, written), check that 'have/has/had' comes before it.

commonMistakes.wrong: I have did my work.
commonMistakes.right: I have done my work.
commonMistakes.why: The past participle of 'do' is 'done', not 'did' (which is the simple past tense form). Keep the three forms straight: do, did, done - 'done' always follows 'have/has/had'.

commonMistakes.wrong: He has ate his lunch.
commonMistakes.right: He has eaten his lunch.
commonMistakes.why: The past participle of 'eat' is 'eaten', not 'ate'. This follows the same pattern as 'write/wrote/written' - many irregular verbs have three completely different forms to memorize.

commonMistakes.wrong: I was knowing him for years before we met properly.
commonMistakes.right: I had known him for years before we met properly.
commonMistakes.why: Since 'know' is a state verb, it should never appear in a continuous form - and because this describes something true before another past event, it needs the past perfect: 'had known'. Combine both rules here: no continuous form for state verbs, and past perfect for the earlier of two past events.

commonMistakes.wrong: She is working here since five years.
commonMistakes.right: She has been working here for five years.
commonMistakes.why: Two things need fixing here: use present perfect continuous ('has been working') instead of present continuous, and use 'for' (not 'since') before a length of time - 'since' is only used with a starting point, like 'since 2018'. Remember: 'for' + duration, 'since' + starting point.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am waiting here since morning.
commonMistakes.right: I have been waiting here since morning.
commonMistakes.why: As before, this needs present perfect continuous because the waiting started in the past and continues now, and 'since morning' correctly marks a starting point, matching the word 'since'. The mistake here is only in the verb tense, not the preposition.

commonMistakes.wrong: I didn't saw him today.
commonMistakes.right: I didn't see him today.
commonMistakes.why: Just like with 'did', the negative 'didn't' already shows past tense, so the verb after it must be in its base form, not past tense. Watch for this exact pattern - 'didn't' + base verb - since it's one of the most frequent tense mistakes.

commonMistakes.wrong: Yesterday, I am going to the market.
commonMistakes.right: Yesterday, I went to the market.
commonMistakes.why: The word 'yesterday' clearly signals the simple past tense, so mixing it with a present tense verb creates a confusing, incorrect sentence. Always match your tense to your time word - past time words need past tense verbs.

commonMistakes.wrong: Tomorrow, I went to the market.
commonMistakes.right: Tomorrow, I will go to the market.
commonMistakes.why: Just as 'yesterday' needs past tense, 'tomorrow' needs future tense ('will go'), since it refers to a time that hasn't happened yet. Get in the habit of checking your time word first, then choosing the matching tense.

commonMistakes.wrong: I have gone there last week.
commonMistakes.right: I went there last week.
commonMistakes.why: Once again, 'last week' is a specific past time marker, so this sentence needs the simple past tense, not the present perfect. If you can point to exactly when something happened, avoid using 'have/has' with it.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am doing this since morning.
commonMistakes.right: I have been doing this since morning.
commonMistakes.why: The word 'since' always signals an action continuing from the past into now, which requires present perfect continuous, not present continuous alone. This is one of the most frequent patterns learners get wrong, so it's worth practicing this exact sentence type.

commonMistakes.wrong: She said she is coming tomorrow.
commonMistakes.right: She said she was coming tomorrow.
commonMistakes.why: When reporting what someone said in the past ('she said'), the tense of the reported part usually shifts back one step too - present becomes past. This pattern is called 'backshifting' in reported speech, and it applies even when the original statement was about the future.

commonMistakes.wrong: He told me he will help me.
commonMistakes.right: He told me he would help me.
commonMistakes.why: Just like other reported speech, 'will' shifts back to 'would' when reporting what someone said in the past. Whenever the reporting verb ('said', 'told') is in the past, shift the tense of the quoted part back by one step.

commonMistakes.wrong: I hope you will enjoyed the party.
commonMistakes.right: I hope you will enjoy the party.
commonMistakes.why: After 'will', the verb must stay in its base form ('enjoy'), never in the past tense ('enjoyed'). This mistake often happens when a sentence has multiple verbs and the writer loses track of which form belongs where.

commonMistakes.wrong: She has already went home.
commonMistakes.right: She has already gone home.
commonMistakes.why: The correct past participle of 'go' is 'gone', not 'went' - remember the three forms: go, went, gone. 'Went' can only be used alone as a simple past tense verb, never after 'have/has/had'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I would went if I had time.
commonMistakes.right: I would go if I had time.
commonMistakes.why: After 'would', the main verb must be in its base form, not the past tense - this is the same rule as after 'will'. Keep this consistent across all modal verbs (will, would, can, could, should): always base form afterward.

commonMistakes.wrong: If I was rich, I will buy a house.
commonMistakes.right: If I were rich, I would buy a house.
commonMistakes.why: This is a Second Conditional sentence describing an unreal, hypothetical present situation, which needs 'were' (not 'was') in the if-clause and 'would' (not 'will') in the result. Learn this whole pattern as a set: If + past tense (were), would + base verb.

commonMistakes.wrong: I wish I am taller.
commonMistakes.right: I wish I were taller.
commonMistakes.why: After 'wish' when talking about something untrue right now, English uses the past tense form 'were' (even with 'I'), showing the wish is unreal or contrary to fact. This pattern feels unusual at first, but it's a fixed structure worth memorizing: wish + past tense, for present wishes.

commonMistakes.wrong: I wish I will pass the exam.
commonMistakes.right: I wish I would pass the exam.
commonMistakes.why: After 'wish' when expressing hope about the future or frustration about someone's current behavior, use 'would', not 'will'. Combine this with the earlier rule: 'wish' pulls the following verb back into a more distant, hypothetical form.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am here since two hours.
commonMistakes.right: I have been here for two hours.
commonMistakes.why: Two corrections are needed: the verb must become present perfect continuous ('have been'), and 'since' should become 'for' because two hours is a duration, not a starting point. Keep repeating this pair until it's automatic: 'for' + duration, 'since' + point in time.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am reaching office by 9 AM daily.
commonMistakes.right: I reach the office by 9 AM daily.
commonMistakes.why: Since this describes a regular habit or routine, it needs the present simple tense, not the present continuous, which is reserved for actions happening right now or temporary situations. Whenever a sentence describes something that happens 'daily', 'usually', or 'every day', use present simple.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am not agreeing with this decision.
commonMistakes.right: I don't agree with this decision.
commonMistakes.why: 'Agree' behaves like a regular action verb here, but it is rarely used in continuous form when expressing an opinion or stance - simple present is far more natural. Use 'don't/doesn't agree' as the standard negative form.

commonMistakes.wrong: He was working here since 2019 before he left.
commonMistakes.right: He had been working here since 2019 before he left.
commonMistakes.why: Because this describes an action continuing up until another past event ('before he left'), it needs the past perfect continuous tense ('had been working'), not the past continuous. Whenever you describe duration leading up to a specific point in the past, use 'had been + verb-ing'.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am thinking you are right.
commonMistakes.right: I think you are right.
commonMistakes.why: When 'think' means 'believe' or 'have an opinion', it's a state verb and should stay in simple present. ('Thinking' in the continuous form is only correct when it means actively processing an idea right now, like 'I am thinking about the problem.')

commonMistakes.wrong: She is seeming tired today.
commonMistakes.right: She seems tired today.
commonMistakes.why: 'Seem' is a state verb describing how something appears, so it's not used in the continuous form. Group 'seem' together with other state verbs like appear, look, and sound when describing impressions.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am owning a car.
commonMistakes.right: I own a car.
commonMistakes.why: 'Own' describes possession, making it a state verb, so it should stay in simple present, not continuous. Whenever the verb describes ownership or belonging (own, possess, belong), avoid the '-ing' form.

commonMistakes.wrong: This bag is belonging to me.
commonMistakes.right: This bag belongs to me.
commonMistakes.why: 'Belong' is another state verb related to possession, and it doesn't take a continuous form. Practice this exact sentence - 'this belongs to me' - since it's a very common phrase in daily conversation.

commonMistakes.wrong: I am preferring tea over coffee.
commonMistakes.right: I prefer tea over coffee.
commonMistakes.why: 'Prefer' expresses a general opinion or state of liking, so it stays in simple present rather than continuous form. This follows the same pattern as like, love, hate, and want - all describe feelings, not actions.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence:
quiz.explanation: 'Know' is a state verb and is not used in the continuous ('-ing') form.
Specific past time words like 'yesterday' need the simple past tense, not present perfect.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence:
options: She has been living here since 2015.
quiz.answer: She has been living here since 2015.
quiz.explanation: 'Since' with an ongoing action needs the present perfect continuous tense.

quiz.question: I will call you when I ___ (arrive).
quiz.explanation: Time clauses with 'when' use the present tense to describe future events, not 'will'.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence:
quiz.explanation: 'Go' is irregular - its past tense form is 'went', not 'goed'.
